revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[az:Hikava məbədi (Saytama)]][[de:Hikawa-Schrein]][[en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)]][[es:Santuario Hikawa]][[eu:Hikawa Jinja]][[fr:Hikawa-jinja]][[hu:Hikava szentély (Szaitama)]][[it:Santuario di Hikawa]][[ja:氷川神社]][[ko:히카와 신사]][[la:Templum Hikawa]][[sh:Hikawa hram (Saitama)]][[tr:Hikawa Tapınağı]][[zh:冰川神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Saitama, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hikawa Shrine
氷川神社 | image = Hikawa-jinja (Saitama), keidai.jpg | caption = Precincts of Hikawa Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Saitama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|55|00|N|139|37|46|E|display=title,inline|region:JP-11_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Hikawa shrine | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
[[Kushinadahime]]
[[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Ashinazuchi
Tenazuchi]] | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|en|Emperor Kōshō|qid=Q312841|lt=Emperor Kōshō|1=Emperor Kōshō}} | established = 473 BC | date_destroyed = | location = 1-407, Takahana-cho, Omiya-ku, Saitama-shi, Saitama, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://musashiichinomiya-hikawa.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = Reitaisai (Annual main festival) (August 1) | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Hikawa Shrine'''|氷川神社|Hikawa-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ōmiya-ku, Saitama|en|Ōmiya-ku, Saitama|qid=Q153629|lt=Ōmiya-ku, Saitama|1=Ōmiya-ku, Saitama}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|qid=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on August 1.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} The district of Omiya, literally "Great Shrine", derives from the special favor shown by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}, who raised Hikawa above all other shrines in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p. 391. It is the head of a network of approximately 280 Hikawa shrines mostly around the Kantō region.Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric. (2005). "Hikawa-jinja" in {{Google books|p2QnPijAEmEC|''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 311|page=311}}. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Hikawa Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]'''|須佐之男命|}}, brother of [[Amaterasu]], god of sea, storms, fields, the harvest, marriage, and love * {{nihongo|'''[[Kushinadahime|Inada-hime no Mikoto]]'''| 稲田姫命|}}, wife of Susanoo, goddess of rice, agriculture, marriage, love, childbirth, and child rearing * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's tradition, the shrine was established during the reign of the legendary {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|en|Emperor Kōshō|qid=Q312841|lt=Emperor Kōshō|1=Emperor Kōshō}} in 473 BC, when the ruling clan of the area, the Musashi ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' migrated to this region from {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, bring with them the worship Susanoo. There is also a legend that the folk hero {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} who injured his leg during his expedition to conquer eastern Japan for the Yamato kingdom, visited the shrine in accordance with the directions of an old man who appeared in a dream. After worshiping, he was able to stand on his own. It is known that the old name of the region, {{Nihongo|Adachi|足立}}, literally meaning "leg stand", was named after this incidence. The pond within the grounds of the shrine is a remnant of {{ill|Minuma|en|Minuma|qid=Q6869769|lt=Minuma|1=Minuma}} and considered to have roots in enshrining the water god of Minuma (a vast swamp that existed until the middle of the Edo period), with the shrine built on a hill or promontory which extended into the swamp. The shrine first appears in the historical record in an entry in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' which was compiled in 901. In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed as a {{nihongo|''myōjin taishi''|名神大社||}}.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 3.] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2011-08-09 During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, {{ill|Taira no Sadamori|en|Taira no Sadamori|qid=Q3514057|lt=Taira no Sadamori|1=Taira no Sadamori}} prayed for victory over {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|en|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|qid=Q1207652|lt=Taira no Masakado}} during the {{ill|Tengyō no Ran|en|Tengyō no Ran|qid=Q2975322|lt=Tengyō no Ran|1=Tengyō no Ran}}. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] ordered Doi Sanehira to rebuild the shrine in 1180, and made many donations. Despite the worship of many prominent people, and the fact that it is the only ''myōjin taishi'' in Musashi, the Hikawa Shrine was not originally regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of the province. The ''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}'' and other contemporary sources give this title to the [[Ono Shrine]] what is now the city of {{ill|Tama, Tokyo|en|Tama, Tokyo|qid=Q213263|lt=Tama|1=Tama, Tokyo}}, whereas the Hikawa Shrine is styled the "san-no-miya" of the province instead. On the other hand, the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} {{nihongo|Dai-Nippon-koku Ichinomiya ki|大日本国一宮記|}} clearly refers to the Hikawa Shrine as the ''ichinomiya''. During the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine prospered greatly from its location in [[:en:Ōmiya-juku|Ōmiya-juku]], which was a [[shukuba|post station]] on the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|qid=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}. Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} relocated from {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} to Tokyo via the Nakasendō in 1868, stopping at this shrine to worship. in 1870, he made it one of ''[[chokusaisha]]'' and visited for a second time. The shrine was also given the Rank of {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} under the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The main shrine structure was renovated in 1882. In 1940, a project financed by the government reconstructed the main shrine structure, the gate tower, and other structures. In 1976, the Large Torii of [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] which had been damaged by {{ill|Lightning|en|Lightning|qid=Q33741|lt=Lightning|1=Lightning}} in 1966 was repaired and relocated to Hikawa Shrine. == History == === Prehistory === According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|en|Emperor Kōshō|qid=Q312841|lt=Emperor Kōshō|1=Emperor Kōshō}}'s reign in April.[http://musashiichinomiya-hikawa.or.jp/about/index.html About Hikawa Shrine] - Musashi Ichinomiya Hikawa Shrine (Accessed July 2, 2018, JST) The "{{ill|Kokuzō Honki|ja|国造本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuzō Honki|1=Kokuzo Hongi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q133833194}}" states that the first {{ill|Muzashi Province Chief|ja|无邪志国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muzashi Province Chief|1=Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504612}}, {{ill|Ani Tamohi-no-Mikoto|ja|兄多毛比命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ani Tamohi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, migrated with the {{ill|Izumo clan|ja|出雲族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo clan|qq=}} to this area during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|en|Emperor Seimu|qid=Q316672|lt=Emperor Seimu|1=Emperor Seimu}} (13th Emperor).{{ill|Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|ja|角川日本地名大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4093011}} 11, Saitama Prefecture. He enshrined his ancestral deity, establishing the shrine as a guardian deity. (Muzashi Province Chiefs) are considered related to the {{ill|Izumo Province Chiefs|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province Chiefs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. There is also a theory that the shrine name "Hikawa" originates from the {{ill|Hii River|en|Hii River|qid=Q11500714|lt=Hiikawa River|1=Hii River}} in Izumo.}} Additionally, a sub-shrine of Hikawa Shrine, "{{ill|Kadokujin Shrine|ja|門客人神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokujin Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アラハバキ|1=Arahabaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285253}}," still stands today to the east of the main shrine hall. Originally called "{{ill|Arahahaba Shrine|ja|荒脛巾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arahahaba Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}," it enshrines the deity {{ill|Arahabaki|ja|アラハバキ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arahabaki|1=Arahabaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285253}} as a guest deity. This shrine is considered the {{ill|Landlord deity|en|Landlord deity|qid=Q4160872|lt=Landowner deity|1=Landlord deity}} of Hikawa Shrine.Otani Taro, "My Ichinomiya Pilgrimage," Seidosha, 2001, p. 69. The currently enshrined deities of Izumo origin are believed to have arrived alongside the {{ill|Muzashi Province Chief's clan|ja|武蔵国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muzashi Province Chief's clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 无邪志国造|1=Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504612}}.Matsumae Ken, "Formation of Japanese Mythology," Hanawa Shobo, p. 186; Hishinuma Isamu, "Ancient Shrines of Musashi," Arimine Shoten, 1972, pp. 71-75; Harashima Reiji, "Hikawa Shrine," in "Japanese Deities and Holy Sites 11 Kanto," edited by Tanikawa Kenichi, Hakusuisha. However, the original deity of the area is thought to be {{ill|Arahabaki|ja|アラハバキ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arahabaki|1=Arahabaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285253}}. A legend also recounts that Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q461258}}, while on his eastern expedition, was injured. He followed a vision of an old man in his dreams and visited the shrine, after which he was able to stand again. This event is said to be the origin of the name "{{ill|Ashida District|ja|足立郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashida District|1=Adachi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11637033}}" (present-day {{ill|Adachi Ward|ja|足立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Adachi Ward|1=Adachi Ward|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636911}}). === Ancient Period === The "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}," compiled in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, records "{{ill|Musashi Province Hikawa Shrine|ja|武蔵国氷川神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" receiving divine Rankings, indicating reverence from the {{ill|Royal court|en|Royal court|qid=Q1134237|lt=Imperial Court of Japan|1=Royal court}}. * {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|qid=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 1 (859), January 27: Raised from {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} to {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}. * Jōgan 5 (863), June 8: Promoted to {{ill|Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正五位下|1=Senior Fifth Rank|lt=Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正五位}}. * Jōgan 7 (865), December 21: Raised to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}}. * Jōgan 11 (869), November 19: Elevated to {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|正四位下|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正四位}}. * {{ill|Gangyō|en|Gangyō|qid=Q747451|lt=Genkei|1=Gangyō}} 2 (878), December 2: Raised to {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|正四位上|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正四位}}. The "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}" (compiled in the mid-Heian period) classifies Hikawa Shrine as a "{{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}," receiving state offerings during the {{ill|monthly festivals|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/monthly festivals|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} and {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niinamesai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}. === Medieval Period === During the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Hikawa Shrine was considered a prominent shrine of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} and received reverence from provincial officials. {{ill|Taira no Sadamori|en|Taira no Sadamori|qid=Q3514057|lt=Taira no Sadamori|1=Taira no Sadamori}} prayed for victory against {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at this shrine, and after emerging victorious, the shrine gained widespread worship from {{ill|Samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1975935}} across the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}. Numerous branch shrines were established along the {{ill|Arakawa|en|Arakawa|qid=Q229488|lt=Arakawa River|1=Arakawa}} and throughout Musashi Province. In {{ill|Jishō|en|Jishō|qid=Q1194671|lt=Jishō|1=Jishō}} 4 (1180), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|qid=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} ordered {{ill|Doi Sanehira|ja|土肥実平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doi Sanehira|1=Sanehira Dohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11423854}} to reconstruct the shrine hall and donated 3,000 {{ill|koku|ja|貫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Japanese kan|qid=Q116911247}} of land.S"hinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō, Hikawa Shrine." In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|qid=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū|1=Kenkyū}} 8 (1197), he dedicated a sacred horse and a divine sword. === Modern Period === On {{ill|October 17|en|October 17|qid=Q2962|lt=October 17|1=October 17}}, {{ill|1868|en|1868|qid=Q7717|lt=1868|1=1868}}, just four days after entering {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo|1=Tokyo Proper|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7473516}}, {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} designated Hikawa Shrine as the guardian shrine of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} and an {{ill|Chokusaisha|en|Chokusaisha|qid=Q175288|lt=Chokusaisha|1=Chokusaisha}}."Imperial Decree: Hikawa Shrine as the Guardian of Musashi Province" - Official Records. He personally visited the shrine on {{ill|October 28|en|October 28|qid=Q2952|lt=October 28|1=October 28}} for the first state ritual in the region. The shrine underwent reconstruction in {{ill|1940|en|1940|qid=Q18633|lt=1940|1=1940}} to commemorate the 2,600th year of Japan’s imperial lineage. The postwar era saw the shrine become a central site for cultural activities, including the annual {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} performance inaugurated in {{ill|1982|en|1982|qid=Q2436|lt=1982|1=1982}} to celebrate the opening of the {{ill|Tōhoku Shinkansen|en|Tōhoku Shinkansen|qid=Q900665|lt=Tōhoku Shinkansen|1=Tōhoku Shinkansen}}. ==Gallery== File:Hikawa-jinja (Saitama), honden.jpg|Honden File:Hikawa-jinja (Saitama), shaden.jpg|Haiden File:Hikawa-jinja (Saitama), buden.jpg|Maidono File:Hikawa-jinja (Saitama), roumon.jpg|Roumon File:Omiya-Hikawa jinja Sando.jpg|Entrance ''torii'' File:氷川神社 神楽殿.JPG|Kagura-den ===Imperial visits to the shrine=== * '''December 11, 1868''': Emperor Meiji's first visit. * '''1870''': The emperor worships at Hikawa. * '''1873''': The emperor moves in state to the shrine. * '''August 31, 1878''': The emperor travels from Tokyo to Hikawa. * '''1896''': The {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|qid=Q157052|lt=Crown Prince Yoshihito|1=Emperor Taishō}} visits the shrine. * '''1917''': The [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Crown Prince Hirohito]] visits the Hikawa. * '''1919''': The [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Regent Hirohito]] (''sesshō'') visits the shrine. * '''1920''': {{ill|Empress Teimei|en|Empress Teimei|qid=Q1197656|lt=Empress Sadako|1=Empress Teimei}} (''kogō'') visits Hikawa. ==Access== * 10-minute walk from {{ill|Kita-Ōmiya Station|en|Kita-Ōmiya Station|qid=Q5367635|lt=Kita-Ōmiya Station|1=Kita-Ōmiya Station}} or {{ill|Ōmiya-kōen Station|en|Ōmiya-kōen Station|qid=Q8081623|lt=Ōmiya-kōen Station|1=Ōmiya-kōen Station}} on the [[:en:Tōbu Noda Line|Tōbu Noda Line]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} * 20-minute walk from [[:en:Omiya Station (Saitama)|Omiya Station]] ({{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} or [[:en:Tōbu Noda Line|Tōbu Noda Line]]) ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system
